text: The robotic arms lift the sigmoid colon to expose the blood vessel supply, while the areas of diverticuli are visually inspected.

The surgeon creates a dissection plane between the mesentery blood supply to the colon and the retroperitoneum, progressing from the medial to the lateral side. The ureter, highlighted with green dye, is visible and carefully avoided during the dissection. The vascular pedicle is isolated.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to coagulate and cut the blood vessel supply to the sigmoid colon. The dissection continues from the medial to the lateral side of the sigmoid colon, with care taken to avoid the ureter, which is visible and marked with green dye.

The surgeon uses a Raytech gauze to push on the medial side through the window, revealing the dissection plane as they mobilize the left colon. The sigmoid colon is freed from its lateral attachments to the abdominal sidewall and retroperitoneum, with careful identification and avoidance of the ureter.

The surgeon observes two separate tubular structures on the left side, identified as duplicated ureters, highlighted with green dye under firefly mode. Both ureters exhibit vermiculation, indicating the passage of urine downstream.

The surgeon continues dissection on the distal sigmoid colon, creating a plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum. Using robotic instruments, the camera is maneuvered underneath the colon with ICG mode to identify the ureters, allowing for precise dissection with enhanced visualization.

The surgeon identifies the distal margin for the sigmoid colon resection and uses a vessel sealer device to divide the mesentery. Additional ICG dye is injected to assess blood flow and vascularity, marking the colon distally for resection. The cutoff point of the green dye is visible at the site where the vascular pedicle was previously taken.

The surgeon introduces a stapler device and staples the distal aspect of the sigmoid colon just above the rectum. A small incision is made to extract the specimen, completing the resection. The anvil of the stapler device is placed in the proximal colon, and an EEA stapler is introduced through the rectum, with the anvil visibly brought through.

The surgeon ensures the orientation of the colon is straight and symmetric before mating the two ends of the stapler. A final injection of ICG dye is administered to confirm good perfusion and blood supply to the anastomosis. The stapler is then fired to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon disengages the stapler and checks the anastomosis for tension, ensuring it is straight. Fluid is filled into the pelvis, and a scope is introduced to perform an air leak test, visually confirming the integrity of the anastomosis.
